Here's where your skills as a practitioner come into play. This is a structured call with an email probably at the end as a follow-up. And the details of what you're discussing is geared towards the data that has been accumulated since the last checkup. The whole point, aside from giving them their new directions, is to prompt to see what they have learned about themselves since the previous check-in. And, namely, we're looking in changes of behavior that they've noticed in themselves that progress them toward their ultimate goal in terms of their changing body composition. So, what you want to know from them is did they change their behavior in any positive ways? What behaviors did they change? And is there any behavior they want to focus on? And if so, now you have some coaching criteria that you need to fulfill. Make sure to prioritize building up the skills required to allow them to gain confidence in the behavior they're looking to build. Again, is there any specific behavior they want to focus on? Great. Prioritize this as a coach. This is what you're going to be discussing until you get to the next in-person meeting. For example, if a person has issues with preparing their meals, then maybe sending a picture of their meal preparation process while they're doing it during their meal preparation day is a great accountability hack for you guys. So, if the person says, you know what, I've been having trouble preparing my meals, maybe if I prepared my meals in bulk on Tuesdays and Fridays, then I would have healthy food available in a convenient way and I wouldn't be as likely to go out. You'd be like, sweet, send me a photo of when you are going to be preparing your meals during the week. And then the person says, oh, great, I'm going to do this on Tuesdays and Fridays and you say, excellent, you put it in the file and then you can harass them on Tuesdays and Fridays and be like, yo, what's the meal prep status? Right? You can do this just about any behavior you want. It could be when a person goes to train. It could be when a person goes out to eat. Maybe they go off the rails in order something silly and then they send you a picture of their grilled chicken and vegetables. Who knows? The world is your oyster. If they take a little bit of encouragement, that's fine. Just use the questions that I have suggested that I had just mentioned. Okay, so after you go through what they've learned about themselves and what you want to focus on, assess the client's communication frequency and tone since the last time they checked in. So are they not communicating with you very much? Are they communicating with you a ton? Do they sound happy? Do they sound sad, aggressive, whatever? What was the overall tone since the last checkup you had? And make sure to encourage more communication if you feel like you require it. Or affirm the client's strong communication skills if you're getting adequate communication from them. So the whole point is that you want to remind them that their feedback is used to generate their customized plan. If you don't know what to focus on through them, then you don't know how to provide tools and tactics that they can employ to help make their lives easier and get to their goal faster. Reinforce that even if they're 99% sure about something, they should still ask you about it. Because what happens if they don't ask you about it? It means they're asking someone else or consulting the Google. And neither of those are going to end up voting well for you, likely. Make sure to reassure them that consistency is key. And make sure to make note of anything that's worth adding to your Q&A database and or the onboarding material for the next time you onboard new clients. Things that you routinely get when people come on. What sort of things can you cover ahead of time to make sure that you don't have as many of those repeat questions? And then last but not least, you go over the new guidelines with this person and you compare the current guidelines to what their previous guidelines were. Even if they were super extra minimum status like at ground zero, it's hey, just weigh yourself and eat off the list. Then the next step is all right. Now we have templated amounts and you're going to use your hand to measure. We are increasing the amount of accuracy that we have, which allows us to increase your level of freedom later when we go to change and introduce more food types. And just affirm that this new set of guidelines is meant to push them toward their goal. Okay, which is fat loss, muscle gain, performance, combination, etc. Reinforce that the more accurate they are with executing the plan you give them, the more accurate you can be with how specific the plan is to them. What I'm trying to say is even the tiniest speck of progress, either in fitness, in mindset or in their body fat is totally super fabulous. And make sure to high five them as hard as you possibly can. Something that I do want to reiterate from a coaching standpoint is that clients may and probably will want change to occur faster. And this is where your skills as a coach will come into play and you'll need to reinforce that the level of accuracy and level of consistency are the primary determinant of speed to results. Any form of results that's positive is very good. And there's no reason to force things to move faster. Things do not work out when that happens. There's always something that you can work on with the client to expect results by virtue of essentially improving their consistency or improving their accuracy and or both. That's how you increase the speed to results. If a person is eating 2000 calories per day and the template is assigning them 1500, then assigning them 1000 calories will not change the fact that they weren't eating 1500 calories to begin with. So make sure that those sorts of things are reconciled and focus on behavior changes that improve the adherence to the plan rather than changing the plan itself to try and expedite the process. I hope that makes sense. If things are stagnant, like there's literally been no movement in the last few weeks, then something really does need to be discussed and review any accountability hacks. Like I said, like sending photos of meals or meal preparation that you both agree on to help stay on track. Make notes in the client's file so that over the next day or so you can percolate any tasks or homework or activity that will benefit this person and possibly a series of activities on how to progress a client into attacking a larger problem. For instance, maybe if a person goes to a restaurant, they always just they go ham on the menu. Like they just go crazy and order the whole menu and dive in and they have a crazy food orgy and don't know what to do. Maybe a progression might be something like maybe for a couple of weeks, they only eat at home. And then after that they graduate to you know what, I like this dish at this restaurant and so they take that dish and then they recreate it at home. I call it eating out at home. And then maybe the next step, step three is to have a weekly restaurant date with a friend and they order something boring. And then they graduate from that to a weekly restaurant date with a friend and get something exciting off of the menu. So be creative. You can't lose the whole point is to get the person engaged. How you get the person engaged honestly doesn't matter a heck of a lot. Just have it be remotely something to do with the behavior that they're having an issue with. The only real thing you need to accomplish is consistent communication during times of need and letting them know that you're there to help them in any way you can.
